title: CCCS - Core questions (original wording)
note: Rewritten July 2026: same knowledge points studied for the exam, original scenarios, wording and distractors. Fully in English.

questions.question: A company wants to roll out the Falcon sensor to a large fleet of AWS EC2 instances using the 1-click sensor deployment. Which two automation methods does this deployment rely on?

options.text: AWS Systems Manager (SSM) and Ansible
Manual SSH sessions on each instance
CloudFormation and Terraform
AWS Config and OpsWorks

questions.explanation: 1-click sensor uses scalable automation: Systems Manager and Ansible install the sensor on many instances without manual individual access.

option_explanations.A: Correct. SSM and Ansible are the automation methods used.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Manual SSH is the opposite of the 1-click goal.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. IaC tools provision infrastructure, they are not the 1-click methods.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. AWS Config assesses compliance and OpsWorks manages configuration, neither installs the sensor here.

questions.memory_tip: 1-click on EC2: SSM and Ansible.

questions.question: Your registry will go through a planned maintenance window and you want Falcon Cloud Security to stop assessing it during that period, without losing the integration or its settings. What should you do?

options.text: Temporarily disable assessments for that registry connection
Delete the registry connection and recreate it after the maintenance
Change the stored credentials so authentication fails during the window
Remove all images from the registry before the maintenance

questions.explanation: The correct procedure is to temporarily pause assessments: the connection and settings stay intact and you re-enable it afterward.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Reversible pause, nothing is lost.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Deleting and recreating is invasive and unnecessary.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Forcing an authentication failure is a workaround, not a procedure.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Removing images is destructive and unrelated to pausing assessments.

questions.memory_tip: Registry maintenance: temporary disable, never delete.

questions.question: Using Identity Analyzer, you must review identity risks across BOTH your AWS and Azure environments, covering excessive permissions and MFA gaps. Which approach satisfies the requirement?

options.text: Filter for overly permissive IAM groups in AWS and analyze user access for MFA and least-privilege violations in Azure AD
Review only expired AWS access keys and skip MFA checks entirely
Audit only Azure AD authentication methods and application registrations, leaving AWS out of scope

questions.explanation: The requirement asks for both providers and both risks: excessive permissions and MFA. Only the first approach covers AWS and Azure at the same time.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Covers both environments and the requested risks.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Scope limited to expired keys and still ignores MFA.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Plausible for Azure, but leaves AWS out of the requirement.

questions.memory_tip: Asked for two providers? The answer must cover both.

questions.question: An organization running AWS, Azure, and GCP is tuning its CSPM to monitor suspicious configurations effectively. Which action is the right way to do this?

options.text: Configure severity levels for monitoring and create custom policies per environment based on predefined rules
Disable all default policies and configure everything manually without policy enforcement
Restrict custom policy creation to AWS only, since other clouds do not support it
Rely exclusively on behavioral attack detection instead of posture policies

questions.explanation: CSPM works with severities and policies: you adjust severity levels and create custom policies per environment based on predefined rules.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Severity + custom policies based on predefined rules.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Turning off default policies removes coverage.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Policy creation is not exclusive to one provider in this scenario.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Behavioral detection complements, it does not replace posture.

questions.memory_tip: CSPM: severity + custom policy on top of predefined rules.

questions.question: While reviewing a container image in the console, you open the image details panel. Which piece of information does it provide?

options.text: Whether the image is a base image
The complete history of every change ever made to the image
A list of all users who have pulled the image
The storage cost of the image in the registry

questions.explanation: The details panel provides relevant image metadata, including whether it is a base image, which helps understand inherited vulnerabilities.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The panel indicates whether the image is a base image.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. There is no complete change history there.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Access auditing is not part of the details panel.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Storage cost is a registry/provider concern.

questions.memory_tip: Image details: metadata, including whether it is a base image.

questions.question: To harden Linux workloads at runtime, you need to enable sensor tamper protection so the Falcon sensor cannot be stopped or removed. Where is this configured?

options.text: In a Prevention policy
In a cloud security posture policy
In a Kubernetes Admission Controller policy
In an image assessment policy

questions.explanation: Tamper protection is a sensor runtime protection feature and is configured in a Prevention Policy.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Prevention Policy controls sensor prevention and protection.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. CSPM assesses cloud configurations.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The KAC acts on workload admission into the cluster.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Image assessment evaluates images, not the sensor.

questions.memory_tip: Sensor tamper protection: Prevention Policy.

questions.question: You operate a self-managed Kubernetes cluster and have full administrative access to the Linux worker nodes. Which sensor deployment is recommended to protect the whole cluster?

options.text: Falcon sensor for Linux deployed as a DaemonSet
Falcon Container sensor injected as a sidecar in every pod
Falcon sensor for Windows on the control plane
A single sensor installed manually on one worker node

questions.explanation: With access to the nodes, the recommendation is the Linux sensor via DaemonSet: one sensor per node, covering every pod in the cluster with kernel-level visibility.

option_explanations.A: Correct. DaemonSet ensures consistent coverage across all nodes.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Sidecar is the alternative for when there is NO access to the node.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The nodes are Linux.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Protecting one node leaves the rest of the cluster exposed.

questions.memory_tip: Have node access? DaemonSet. Don't? Sidecar.

questions.question: Which statement describes a real benefit of CrowdStrike's cloud security products for infrastructure and workloads?

options.text: They secure workloads across cloud and on-premises environments
They provision and deploy the cloud infrastructure for you
They manage the auto-scaling of cloud-native workloads

questions.explanation: The platform protects workloads wherever they are, in the cloud or on-premises. Provisioning and scaling are roles played by other tools.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Protection of workloads in any environment.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Provisioning is a role for IaC (Terraform, CloudFormation).
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Scaling is a role for the provider and orchestrators.

questions.memory_tip: CrowdStrike protects. It does not provision, does not scale.

questions.question: While registering an AWS account in Falcon Cloud Security, which optional capability is recommended to strengthen your posture around sensitive data?

options.text: Data Security Posture Management (DSPM)
Application Security Posture Management (ASPM)
AWS Trusted Advisor

questions.explanation: Enabling DSPM during onboarding adds discovery and classification of sensitive data, exposure, and access risks across the account's data services.

option_explanations.A: Correct. DSPM handles sensitive data posture.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. ASPM is application posture, a different scope.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Trusted Advisor is an AWS service, not part of Falcon registration.

questions.memory_tip: Sensitive data during onboarding: turn on DSPM.

questions.question: Your organization prioritizes image vulnerabilities using CrowdStrike's AI-driven rating that predicts the likelihood of exploitation. Which dashboard column is that?

options.text: ExPRT rating
CVSS score
CVE ID
Severity

questions.explanation: ExPRT is CrowdStrike's predictive, dynamic rating, powered by threat intelligence, that prioritizes by the actual likelihood of exploitation.

option_explanations.A: Correct. ExPRT = CrowdStrike's predictive/dynamic rating.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. CVSS is an industry standard and static, not predictive.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. CVE ID is just the vulnerability identifier.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Severity is a general classification, not the probability of exploit.

questions.memory_tip: Predictive and dynamic = ExPRT.

questions.question: You suspect there are cloud assets running without any CrowdStrike agent. In the Cloud Security Assets view, which value of the Managed by filter reveals them?

options.text: Unmanaged
None
Snapshot
Customer

questions.explanation: Unmanaged identifies assets discovered by the cloud integration that do not have the Falcon sensor installed, revealing coverage gaps.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Unmanaged = no agent.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. None is not the value used for this.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Snapshot refers to agentless scanning, not management status.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Customer does not indicate an absent agent.

questions.memory_tip: No agent: Managed by filter = Unmanaged.

questions.question: You opened a misconfiguration detection and need the recommended steps to fix it. Where are they?

options.text: In the Detection details panel, under the Remediation section
In the policy details that generated the detection
In the description field of the detection

questions.explanation: The Remediation section of the details panel provides the fix guidance. The description explains the risk; the policy defines the rule.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Remediation = how to fix it.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The policy describes the rule and criteria.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Description explains the problem, not the remediation steps.

questions.memory_tip: Description explains, Remediation fixes.

questions.question: A workload is admitted to your cluster with a container granted powerful network capabilities such as NET_ADMIN. What type of detection does this generate?

options.text: Kubernetes misconfiguration
Drift indicator
Unidentified container
Generic alert

questions.explanation: Dangerous capabilities are part of the workload's security context: it is a Kubernetes misconfiguration, not a runtime change.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Excessive privilege in the manifest = K8s misconfiguration.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Drift is a divergence from the image at runtime.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. It is not a container identification issue.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. There is a specific category for this.

questions.memory_tip: Dangerous capability in the manifest: K8s misconfiguration.

questions.question: The Falcon container sensor was deployed to a Kubernetes cluster but no telemetry is arriving in the console. Following best practices, what is the FIRST troubleshooting step?

options.text: Verify the sensor pod is running and has network connectivity to the CrowdStrike cloud
Recreate the entire Kubernetes cluster and reinstall the sensor
Resize the cluster nodes to add resources
Disable the sensor and monitor the containers manually

questions.explanation: Start with the basics: is the sensor pod Running? Do DNS, HTTPS, proxy, and firewall allow communication with CrowdStrike's endpoints?

option_explanations.A: Correct. Pod status + connectivity is the first check.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Recreating the cluster is an extreme measure without diagnosis.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Resources can be investigated after the basics.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Disabling the sensor abandons protection without solving anything.

questions.memory_tip: No telemetry: check pod Running + network first.

questions.question: You just connected a new container registry to Falcon Cloud Security. Which connection status confirms it was set up successfully?

options.text: Operational
Pending
Assessed

questions.explanation: Operational indicates the connection was validated and is functional. Pending is validation in progress; Assessed describes images, not connections.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Connection validated and active.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Pending = still validating.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Assessed is the state of an evaluated image.

questions.memory_tip: Good connection: Operational. Evaluated image: Assessed.

questions.question: To reduce noise and assign ownership, you want to organize cloud resources into cloud groups. On what basis are these groups built?

options.text: Common properties shared with other assets, such as account, tags, or environment
The real-time visibility status of each account
A fixed assessment schedule applied to all accounts
The misconfigurations found in the latest CSPM assessment

questions.explanation: Cloud groups group assets by common properties: account, region, tags, environment, owner. This reduces noise and defines ownership.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Grouping by shared attributes.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Visibility status does not define groups.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Scheduling does not organize assets.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Findings are a result, not a grouping criterion.

questions.memory_tip: Cloud groups: shared properties.

questions.question: Which capability does the Kubernetes Admission Controller provide in Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: It evaluates workloads as they enter the cluster and surfaces containers that are unprotected or violate policy
It scans registry images for vulnerabilities on a schedule
It deploys the runtime sensor to the cluster nodes automatically
It performs vulnerability scans inside running containers

questions.explanation: The Admission Controller acts at admission time: it evaluates workloads against policies and surfaces unprotected or non-compliant containers.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Admission-time evaluation + discovery of unprotected workloads.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Registry scanning belongs to Image Assessment.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Deploying the sensor to the nodes is done by the DaemonSet/Operator.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The KAC is not a runtime vulnerability scanner.

questions.memory_tip: KAC: cluster gatekeeper, sees who enters unprotected.

questions.question: A registered AWS account shows an ORANGE dot on the Real-time visibility and detection indicator. What does this mean?

options.text: IOAs are active, but some regions are not capturing CloudTrail management events
IOAs are inactive and no CloudTrail events are being received at all
A critical IOA was just detected in the account
The EventBridge rules are misconfigured and detection is fully down

questions.explanation: Orange means partial coverage: detection works, but not all regions are sending CloudTrail management events.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Active, but with undiscovered regions.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Total absence would be a failure state, not a partial one.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The color reflects the integration's state, not detections.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. A total detection failure is more severe than what orange indicates.

questions.memory_tip: Orange: working, but missing a region.

questions.question: Management wants a single daily report summarizing security posture across AWS, Azure, and GCP. Which report delivers this?

options.text: Cloud Security Posture Summary
Cloud Security Compliance Overview
Cloud Security Posture CVEs
Cloud Security Posture for Misconfigurations

questions.explanation: The Posture Summary consolidates the posture of all three providers daily into a single view.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Daily multicloud overview.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Focused on compliance.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Focused on vulnerabilities.

questions.memory_tip: Daily view of the 3 providers: Posture Summary.

questions.question: Your workloads run on Amazon ECS with the Fargate launch type. Which Falcon sensor protects these workloads?

options.text: Falcon Container Sensor for Linux
Falcon sensor for Linux installed on the host
Falcon sensor for Windows
No sensor supports ECS Fargate

questions.explanation: On Fargate there is no accessible host, so protection comes from the Falcon Container Sensor for Linux, which runs alongside the container in user space.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Container sensor is the path for ECS Fargate.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. There is no host access on Fargate.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The environment is Linux.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Support exists, via the Container sensor.

questions.memory_tip: ECS Fargate: Container Sensor for Linux.

questions.question: You are hunting for Persistence activity in your AWS environment using cloud IOAs. Which pair of filters narrows the results to exactly that?

options.text: Attack type + Cloud provider
MITRE Tactic and Technique + Service
Attack type + Service
Severity + Region

questions.explanation: Attack type selects Persistence and Cloud provider narrows it to AWS, exactly the scope requested.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Attack type + provider covers the requirement.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Service narrows to a specific service, not the whole provider.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Missing the provider filter.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Severity and region do not identify Persistence nor AWS as the provider.

questions.memory_tip: Persistence in AWS: Attack type + Cloud provider.

questions.question: To install Image Assessment at Runtime (IAR), an engineer needs to generate the required API key. Which role can generate it?

options.text: Falcon Admin
CSPM Reader
IAR Admin
Kubernetes and Containers Manager

questions.explanation: Generating the IAR API key requires the Falcon Admin role, since it involves tenant API credential permissions.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Falcon Admin has the permission to generate the key.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Reader is read-only.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Not the role with this generation permission.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The K8s scope does not include generating this API key.

questions.memory_tip: IAR API key: Falcon Admin.

questions.question: With workloads spread across multiple cloud providers, you need to evaluate security controls and spot misconfigurations and high-risk practices. Which capability do you use?

options.text: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)
Network Threat Detection
Container Drift Detection

questions.explanation: Evaluating multicloud controls and configurations to find misconfigurations is CSPM's core function.

option_explanations.A: Correct. CSPM = multicloud posture and misconfigurations.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Network threat detection is a different scope.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Drift is a container change at runtime.

questions.memory_tip: Multicloud misconfiguration: CSPM.

questions.question: You are worried about credentials being exposed inside your Kubernetes cluster. Which IOM rule alerts on this kind of exposure?

options.text: Sensitive data in secretKeyRef
Container with many capabilities
Workload without securityContext

questions.explanation: The rule identifies sensitive data, such as passwords, tokens, and keys, referenced via secretKeyRef in Kubernetes workloads.

option_explanations.A: Correct. secretKeyRef is directly tied to Secrets and credentials.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Capabilities deal with container privileges.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Missing securityContext is a privilege misconfiguration, not credential exposure.

questions.memory_tip: Exposed credential in K8s: secretKeyRef.

questions.question: An adversary group is known for operating in cloud accounts with valid stolen credentials, escalating privileges, and then erasing traces of their activity. Which TWO Indicators of Attack together match this behavior in AWS?

options.text: Inline administrator policy (*:*) applied to an IAM principal + CloudTrail logging disabled
Network ACLs allowing ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 + subnets with automatic public IP assignment
API call from a hacking-related operating system + security group opened to the public internet
Certificate added to an application registration + consent granted to an application for directory data

questions.explanation: An inline administrator policy (*:*) on a principal indicates privilege escalation; disabling CloudTrail is defense evasion to erase traces, exactly the behavior described.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Escalation + erasing evidence, the pair that describes the scenario.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. These are network misconfigurations, not attack behavior.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Suspicious, but does not include removal of evidence.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Typical Entra ID/Azure activities, outside the AWS scenario.

questions.memory_tip: Escalate + erase traces: inline admin policy + CloudTrail disabled.
